putting a playable snake game in a fake terminal
. The fun constraint: the "screen" is just a
element in a Vue component. No canvas, no WebGL, just monospace text re-rendered 8 times per second.
the game is a string factory
A game here is a function that receives two callbacks: one to draw a frame, one to report the final output when the game ends.
Every tick, snake builds the whole board as a string — box-drawing characters for the walls,
for the food — and hands it to
, and because the font is monospace, the "pixels" line up perfectly:
Diffing a full-frame string sounds wasteful, but the frame is ~700 characters. The DOM update is nothing; your GPU yawns.
The terminal normally routes keys to an
. While a game is active, the input is hidden and a window-level listener feeds keys to the game instead:
for keys it consumed, so arrows stop scrolling the page but
end the game instead of closing the terminal — that one took a bug report from myself to get right.
When you die, the terminal prints:

game over — the snake has encountered a segfault
final score: 120
new high score! previous best: 80
, because if you're going to lose to a snake made of Unicode blocks, the least I can do is remember it.
. My high score is embarrassing, please beat it.

a game loop, a <pre> tag, and stealing the keyboard from vue — no canvas required.
rebuilding my portfolio after 4 years of "coming soon(ish)"
For four years, my personal website consisted of a hero, six project cards, and the sentence
"My personal slice of the web is still under construction and will be live if I can find some time to finish it ;)"
I never found the time. So instead of finishing v1, I deleted it.
. Bulma might seem like an odd choice next to the utility-css crowd, but v1 moved everything to CSS variables, which makes theming almost free:
element — Bulma swaps every variable, and anything custom reads
so it follows along. Even the canvas animations read their colors from the CSS variables at runtime.
The gimmick I actually wanted to build: press
and you get a terminal. It's not an emulator — it's a Vue component and a composable with a command map:

so the navbar button, the footer link and the overlay all talk to the same terminal. Commands can navigate the router, toggle the theme, fetch the GitHub API, or take over the keyboard entirely — which is how

), but the rule I set myself: every easter egg has to be discoverable from a hint somewhere else. The console hints at the terminal, the terminal hints at the hidden commands, the hidden commands hint at... you get it.
If you find one that isn't hinted at anywhere, that's a bug. Or is it?
